name: TD Ameritrade
description_short: Legacy full-service brokerage now merged with Charles Schwab, offering commission-free trading, powerful tools, and extensive research.
description_long:
## The TD Ameritrade Account You Cannot Open Anymore

The first thing to know about TD Ameritrade in 2026 is that it no longer exists as a place to open a new brokerage account. TD Ameritrade, Inc. has been acquired by Charles Schwab, and every client account has been migrated onto Schwab's platform. If you land on tdameritrade.com today, you are redirected to Schwab. So a review of TD Ameritrade is really a review of what its legacy means at Schwab, whether existing customers got a raw deal, and whether the pieces that made TD Ameritrade worth choosing in this US brokers category, namely thinkorswim and the education library, still work the way they used to.

That framing matters because most people searching for TD Ameritrade in 2026 fall into one of two groups. Either they were TDA clients and want to understand what changed after their account moved, or they read older reviews that praised TD Ameritrade and want to know if the newer Schwab-branded version is the same product with a different logo. Both are legitimate questions, and the honest answer to each is nuanced.

## Why thinkorswim Still Anchors This Review

thinkorswim is the reason TD Ameritrade earned its reputation with active traders, options sellers and futures traders, and it is the single asset Schwab most obviously wanted from the deal. The desktop platform, the web version and the mobile app are still available, still free with a funded brokerage account, and still branded thinkorswim rather than something Schwab. The order routing, charting engine, thinkScript custom indicators, backtesting via OnDemand and the paper trading account all survived the migration.

If you were a TD Ameritrade client using thinkorswim to sell weekly options spreads, run pairs trades or chart futures, the actual trading workflow you built up over years is intact. That is the strongest continuity argument for the merged product. It is also the main reason a self-directed active trader can still recommend the Schwab-Ameritrade combination as a US broker in 2026, even without new TD Ameritrade accounts being available.

## What Actually Changed for TD Ameritrade Clients

Schwab describes the transition in soft language, but the practical changes are worth naming plainly. Every TD Ameritrade login was retired. Clients had to set up new Schwab credentials. Cost basis, account numbers, tax lots and open positions were migrated in batches over several conversion weekends, and some traders reported temporary display issues, missing transaction histories in the first week after their batch moved, and delays getting margin or options trading levels re-approved.

Most of these issues resolved, but they were not zero-impact. Anyone reviewing TD Ameritrade honestly has to acknowledge that the transition period caused real disruption, especially for traders who depended on active margin, complex options approvals or API keys that had to be reissued under Schwab. The commission-free stock and ETF pricing carried over, and the $0.65 per options contract fee remains the standard. Futures and forex pricing sits inside the Schwab schedule now rather than TD Ameritrade's own.

## The Real Cost of Trading This Broker

The headline is still $0 commissions on online stock and ETF trades, and TD Ameritrade legacy customers get the same Schwab satisfaction guarantee that new Schwab clients see advertised. Options remain $0.65 per contract, which is competitive with most large US brokers but not the cheapest, and it stacks up quickly if you trade high-volume options strategies where per-contract fees dominate.

For a trader running 40 contract iron condors twice a week, $0.65 per leg is a meaningful monthly line item and worth comparing directly against Tastytrade or Interactive Brokers before staying by default. Mutual funds, bonds, CDs, money market funds and cryptocurrency are all available inside the Schwab shell that TD Ameritrade migrated into, so product breadth is not the constraint. What is not verified in the source material Schwab publishes on the transition page is the exact futures commission per contract, forex spreads, or the specific margin interest tiers, so anyone treating those as decision factors should pull the current Schwab pricing sheet directly rather than trust a legacy TD Ameritrade number.

## Education and Research: The Quiet Reason People Stayed

One of the underrated things about the old TD Ameritrade brand was the depth of its education and research library, including the immersive curriculum inside thinkorswim, the Investools content, and third-party research from Morningstar, CFRA and Credit Suisse formats layered inside the platform. Schwab has folded most of this into its own education section under the Schwab Trading Powered by Ameritrade banner.

The practical outcome is that the material is still there, still free with an account, and still one of the strongest reasons for a newer trader to pick this broker over a bare-bones app. The tradeoff is that navigation changed, some content was renamed or restructured, and clients who bookmarked specific TD Ameritrade learning paths had to re-find them under Schwab's information architecture. That is annoying rather than fatal, but if your entire self-education workflow lived at specific TDA URLs, expect a rebuilding week.

## Where the Merged Product Falls Short

There are three honest weaknesses that a review of TD Ameritrade in its current form has to name. First, automated trading in the fully hands-off sense, meaning strategy bots that route orders without you, is not supported natively; thinkorswim's alerts, scripts and conditional orders are powerful but they are not a strategy engine. Second, there is no built-in trade journaling layer, so traders serious about performance review still need TraderSync, Tradervue or Edgewonk alongside the account. Third, the AI-driven trade analysis features that newer platforms lean on are absent here; thinkorswim's edge is charting and options analytics, not machine learning on your fills.

On top of those product gaps, the transition itself is a soft weakness. Some clients had margin buying power recalculated under Schwab's model, and a small subset of specialty account types, particularly some trust and small business account structures, needed manual re-paperwork rather than a clean electronic migration. None of this is a reason to leave if you were happy on TD Ameritrade, but it is worth knowing before you send a new dollar in.

## Who Should Still Choose This Broker

The legacy TD Ameritrade product, now Schwab Trading Powered by Ameritrade, still makes sense for active US-based traders who want thinkorswim as their primary platform, options sellers who value the analytics and paper trading environment, self-directed investors who want research and education inside the same login as their portfolio, and futures traders who like keeping equities, options and futures in one account. It is less well-suited for pure algo traders needing native strategy automation, day traders chasing the absolute lowest per-contract options fees, and anyone who was building around specific TD Ameritrade API endpoints without wanting to migrate to Schwab's implementation.

If you were happy at TD Ameritrade, the honest recommendation is to stay, learn where things moved in the Schwab shell, and re-benchmark options pricing against Tastytrade if your volume is high. If you were considering opening a new TD Ameritrade account, you cannot; the choice is now between Schwab (which inherited the product), Fidelity, Interactive Brokers or a specialty options broker like Tastytrade. Judged as the platform it became, and not the brand it used to be, TD Ameritrade at Schwab remains one of the more defensible picks in the US brokers category, mostly because thinkorswim survived intact.
pros: thinkorswim desktop, web and mobile survived the Schwab migration intact
$0 online stock and ETF commissions carried over
Deep education and research library still free with a funded account
Paper trading and thinkScript custom indicators remain available
Access to more than 400 physical Schwab branches for in-person support
Wide product range: stocks, options, futures, forex, crypto, bonds, funds
cons: No new TD Ameritrade accounts; you are opening a Schwab account instead
$0.65 per options contract adds up quickly for high-volume options traders
Migration caused temporary account, margin and options-level disruptions
No native automated strategy execution or built-in trade journaling
No AI-driven trade analysis features inside the platform
best_for: Active traders who rely on thinkorswim
Options traders who value analytics and paper trading
Self-directed investors who want research and education in one login
Futures traders who like a multi-asset US brokerage account
Existing TD Ameritrade clients continuing at Schwab
meta_title: TD Ameritrade Review 2026: Now at Schwab, Honest Take
meta_description: TD Ameritrade is now Charles Schwab. Honest 2026 review of what changed for clients, thinkorswim's status, real costs, and who should still use it.
company_background: TD Ameritrade was founded in 1975 and grew into one of the largest US retail brokers before being acquired by Charles Schwab. The brand is headquartered in Omaha, Nebraska, and its trading technology, especially thinkorswim, is now operated under Schwab Trading Powered by Ameritrade. No new TD Ameritrade accounts can be opened; all clients are now Schwab clients.
